name: Multiplying 2-Digit by 2-Digit — Four Patches
whatIsIt: To multiply 24 × 15, break BOTH numbers by place value: (20+4) × (10+5). The rope trick now cuts the orchard into FOUR patches: 20×10, 20×5, 4×10, 4×5. Multiply each patch, add all four — 200+100+40+20 = 360. Every big multiplication is just small table facts wearing place-value coats.
whyNeeded: Two-digit × two-digit shows up everywhere real quantities meet: 24 boxes of 15 laddus, 12 rows of 18 chairs, 25 days of 40 rupees saved. It's also where the column method's 'add a zero on the second line' finally makes SENSE — the four patches explain it.
realLifeUses: Party order: 24 boxes with 15 sweets each
Savings: ₹25 saved every day for 31 days
School: 32 pages of homework for 14 students to print

vocabulary.meaning: Split BOTH factors by place value: 24×15 = (20+4)×(10+5).
The four patch answers that add to the total.
The column method's second line ends in 0 because it multiplies by TENS.

story.title: The Hillside Tea Estate
story.text: On the misty hillside tea estate, there are 24 rows of tea bushes, with 15 bushes in each row. 'How many bushes in all?' asks Meena. The manager splits both numbers: '24 is 20+4, and 15 is 10+5. That makes four patches: 20×10=200, 20×5=100, 4×10=40, 4×5=20. Add them all — 200+100+40+20 = 360 bushes!'
story.extractedProblem: 24 rows of 15 tea bushes — how many bushes?

visual.caption: Two ropes → four patches. EVERY piece of 24 multiplies EVERY piece of 15. Add all four patches.

teachingGallery.title: Four patches for 2-digit × 2-digit
teachingGallery.note: Split BOTH numbers into tens and ones — that makes four easy multiplications.

standardMethod.summary: Break both numbers by place value; multiply every piece by every piece; add the four partials.

alternateMethods.name: Column method decoded
alternateMethods.whenToUse: Exams — it's the four patches in two lines.
steps: Line 2: 24 × 10 = 240 — write it with its 0, because it's TENS! (the two left patches: 200+40)
The 'magic zero' isn't magic — line 2 multiplies by ten!

alternateMethods.name: Friendly-number adjust
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When one factor is near a round number.

alternateMethods.name: Vedic: near 100 below (Nikhilam)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When both numbers are just below 100 (like 97 × 96).
steps: Find each number's deficit from 100: 97 is −3, 96 is −4.
Cross-subtract for the left part: 97 − 4 = 93 (same as 96 − 3).
Multiply the deficits for the right part, as two digits: 3 × 4 = 12.

alternateMethods.name: Vedic: near 100 above (Nikhilam)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When both numbers are just above 100 (like 103 × 104).
steps: Find each number's excess over 100: 103 is +3, 104 is +4.
Cross-ADD for the left part: 103 + 4 = 107 (same as 104 + 3).
Multiply the excesses for the right part, two digits: 3 × 4 = 12.

alternateMethods.name: Vedic: last digits add to 10 (Antyayordasake'pi)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When both numbers share the same tens digit and their units add to 10 (like 43 × 47).
steps: Check: same front digit (4 and 4) and units add to 10 (3 + 7 = 10).
Left part: front × next number up → 4 × 5 = 20.
Right part: multiply the two units, as two digits → 3 × 7 = 21.

workedExamples.problem: A hall has 12 rows of 18 chairs. How many chairs?
Ravi computed 24 × 15 with only TWO patches: '20×10 = 200 and 4×5 = 20 → 220!' Which patches did he skip?
steps: He multiplied outer pieces only — but EVERY piece meets EVERY piece!
The area picture shows his two empty patches immediately — and 220 fails the ≈375 estimate.

trickPractice.intro: Split both numbers into tens and ones — four little products you can do in your head.

commonMistakes.mistake: Multiplying only 20×10 and 4×5, skipping the cross patches (20×5, 4×10).
commonMistakes.fix: Two ropes make FOUR patches, not two! Draw the rectangle: every row-piece must meet every column-piece. Count your partials — a 2×2 break always gives 4.

commonMistakes.mistake: In the column method, writing line 2 without its ending zero (24×1 instead of 24×10).
commonMistakes.fix: Line 2 multiplies by the TENS digit — that's ×10-something! Its answer lives one place left, so it ends in 0. Say it aloud: 'twenty-four times TEN'.

commonMistakes.mistake: Accepting any total without the estimate guard (220 or 3600 for 24×15).
commonMistakes.fix: Round first: 24×15 is about 25×15 = 375. Any answer far from 375 — like 220 or 3600 — is instantly suspicious. Two seconds of estimating saves the whole sum.

easy.q: Breaking 24 × 15 with two ropes gives how many patches?
hintLadder: One rope cuts rows, one cuts columns.

medium.q: A party order: 21 boxes of 14 sweets. Total sweets?
In the column method for 24 × 15, the second line ends in 0 because…

options.label: It multiplies by the TENS (×10), so it sits one place left

medium.answer: It multiplies by the TENS (×10), so it sits one place left

challenge.q: Savings plan: ₹25 every day for 31 days. Total rupees? (Try the friendly way: 25×30 + 25.)
For 24 × 15, Ravi got 220. The estimate says ≈ 375. What should he do FIRST?

options.label: Trust the estimate — recheck for skipped patches (he missed 100+40!)
Trust his answer — estimates are rough

challenge.answer: Trust the estimate — recheck for skipped patches (he missed 100+40!)

questions.q: How many partial products does (20+4) × (10+5) create?
hintLadder: Each piece of the first meets each piece of the second.

teachBackPrompt: Draw Multi's two-rope orchard for 12 × 13 on paper for someone at home: show all FOUR patches, add them, then reveal how the school column method hides the same four patches in two lines.

revisionCard.summary: 2-digit × 2-digit: break BOTH numbers, make FOUR patches (every piece × every piece), add all partials. 24×15 = 200+100+40+20 = 360. Column method line 2 ends in 0 because it's ×TENS. Estimate first (≈375) — it catches skipped patches!

gameMission.title: The Mega Orchard Contracts
gameMission.brief: Huge planting orders are flooding in! Stretch both ropes, fill all four patches, and pass every order through the estimate gate. Ravi's two-patch shortcuts keep leaving orchards half-empty — audit him!

realLifeProject: Real order math: find a snack box at home (biscuits per packet × packets per box, or similar). Compute the total with four patches AND with columns — do they agree? Show a parent both ways.

mentalMathMethod.name: Break apart (area method)
steps: Split both numbers into tens + ones.

abacusMethod.name: Four partial products on the rods
abacusMethod.whenToUse: To multiply two-digit numbers on the abacus.
steps: Add the two cross products (tens×ones) in the tens place.
Add tens×tens in the hundreds place; read the total.

visual.caption: The four partial products, added in their columns.

vedicMethod.name: Vertically and Crosswise (Urdhva-Tiryagbhyam)
vedicMethod.whenToUse: The fast Vedic way for 2×2 digits.
steps: UNITS: multiply the two units vertically (right).
MIDDLE: cross-multiply the two pairs and add.
TENS: multiply the two tens vertically (left). Carry as you go.

rememberIt.hook: Long multiplication: multiply by ones, then tens (shift a place), add

studentQuestions.q: Why put a 0 on the second line?
studentQuestions.a: The second digit is TENS, so its answer is ten times bigger - the 0 shifts it into the tens place.
